title: Terse.jl
text:
Installation
Terse.jl provides the @types macro for defining Julia type hierarchies concisely.

title: Terse.jl
text:
Quickstart

using Terse

@types Shape > (
    Circle(radius::Float64),
    Rectangle(width::Float64, height::Float64)
)

@show supertype(Circle), supertype(Rectangle)
Circle(1.0), Rectangle(3.0, 4.0)

(supertype(Circle), supertype(Rectangle)) = (Main.Notebook.Shape, Main.Notebook.Shape)


(Circle(1.0), Rectangle(3.0, 4.0))


See Getting Started for a full overview of all supported syntax forms.

title: Getting Started
Getting Started
text:
Abstract Type with Subtypes
Use > to define an abstract type and its concrete subtypes together:

@types Animal > (
    Cat(lives::Int),
    Dog(name::String)
)

supertype(Cat), supertype(Dog)

(Animal, Animal)



Cat(9), Dog("Rex")

(Cat(9), Dog("Rex"))

title: Getting Started
text:
Parametric Types
Type parameters and bounds work as normal Julia syntax inside {}:

@types Envelope{T} > (
    Plain{T}(value::T),
    Labelled{T, S <: AbstractString}(value::T, label::S)
)

Labelled{Int, String}(42, "answer")

Labelled{Int64, String}(42, "answer")

title: Getting Started
text:
Nested Hierarchies
A subtype can itself be an abstract type with its own subtypes by using > again. Bare names (no parentheses) produce zero-field structs that inherit the parent’s type parameters:

@types Animal2{T} > (
    Mammal{T}(family::T),
    Invertebrate{T} > (
        Worm,
        Insect{T, I <: Integer}(legs::I, family::T)
    )
)

supertype(Invertebrate{Float64}), supertype(Worm{Float64})

(Animal2{Float64}, Invertebrate{Float64})



Worm{String}(), Insect{Float64, Int8}(6, 1.0)

(Worm{String}(), Insect{Float64, Int8}(6, 1.0))
